title: Gothic Textura lowercase
subtitle: Writing the compressed, vertical lowercase alphabet of Gothic Black Letter
excerpt: Gothic Textura Quadrata is the formal script of medieval manuscripts from northern Europe. Its characteristic tightly-compressed verticals and diamond-tipped strokes are built entirely from a set of six basic pen moves. This tutorial introduces the lowercase alphabet from the stroke up.
sourceNotes: Margaret Shepherd, Learn Calligraphy (2001, Broadway Books), Gothic chapter. Also draws on Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906, John Hogg), the original modern calligraphy text. Johnston's 1906 work is public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: The most formal variety of Gothic Black Letter, distinguished by flat horizontal finishing strokes (quadrangular feet) at the base and head of each vertical stroke, giving the page a woven, textile-like texture.
The angle at which the broad-edge nib is held relative to the horizontal writing line. Gothic Textura uses a 40-45° angle; changing this angle alters the thick-to-thin stroke ratio.
The height of the lowercase letters, measured in nib-widths. Gothic Textura lowercase is typically written at 4 nib-widths x-height, stack the nib four times to set the writing lines.

content.text: looks complex but is built from very few strokes: a vertical with flat entry and exit hairlines, a compressed arch, and a small diamond serif. Setting the
correctly is the single most important step before writing the first letter.

The Brause Bandzug nib is 1.5 mm or 2 mm wide. Use it to draw a staircase of nib-width squares: make four stacked squares. That height is your x-height. Add an ascender zone of three nib-widths above, and a descender zone of three nib-widths below. Rule these lines lightly with a pencil on a guide sheet; tape the guide sheet to the light box and lay cartridge over it to write through.
Hold the pen so the nib edge makes a 40° angle with the baseline. At this
, a straight downstroke gives a thick stroke and a horizontal pull gives a thin one. Don't roll the pen, keep the angle fixed throughout every stroke on every letter.

The minim is the single vertical that builds n, m, u, i, and the body of almost every other letter. Enter at the x-height line with a tiny horizontal hairline pull to the right, then pull straight down to the baseline, then exit with a tiny horizontal hairline to the right. The entry and exit strokes are made with the full nib edge and must be precise, they produce the flat quadrangular feet that give the script its name.

Practice the sequence in this order: i → u → n → m → a → d → g → b → p → q → c → e → o → f → t → h → k → l → r → s → v → w → x → y → z. Group i/u/n/m as pure minim-only letters; then d/b/p/q as minim-plus-arch; then c/e/o as oval forms. Space letters so the white internal space inside each letter matches the white counter space between letters.

items.problem: Feet look ragged rather than clean and flat
items.cause: Nib entered the hairline stroke at the wrong angle or moved too fast
items.fix: Slow down dramatically for the entry and exit hairlines. The nib should pause for a fraction of a second before and after each vertical stroke.

items.cause: The compression ratio (width to height) is not consistent
items.fix: The body of each minim should be about half its height in width. Mark a dot with the nib at the halfway point and aim for that consistently before writing full letters.

items.problem: Ink blobs at the top of each vertical
items.cause: Too much ink on the nib; nib touched the paper before starting the hairline
items.fix: Blot excess ink on a cloth before each stroke. Approach the paper from above, not from the side.
